LITERATURE CARD
DeepDRiD: Diabetic Retinopathy — Grading and Image Quality Estimation Challenge
1. Bibliographic Metadata
Full Citation (APA 7): Liu, R., Wang, X., Wu, Q., Dai, L., Fang, X., Yan, T., Son, J., Tang, S., Li, J., Gao, Z., Galdran, A., Poorneshwaran, J.M., Liu, H., Wang, J., Chen, Y., Porwal, P., Tan, G.S.W., Yang, X., Dai, C., Song, H., Chen, M., Li, H., Jia, W., Shen, D., Sheng, B., & Zhang, P. (2022). DeepDRiD: Diabetic Retinopathy—Grading and Image Quality Estimation Challenge. Patterns, 3(6), 100512. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.patter.2022.100512
DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.patter.2022.100512
Journal: Patterns (Cell Press)
Year: 2022
Publication Type: Benchmark / Challenge Descriptor (empirical)
Research Domain: Diabetic retinopathy deep learning; image quality assessment; DR grading; ultra-widefield fundus imaging
2. Study Type Classification
•	Benchmark study (challenge-based evaluation of multiple algorithms)
•	CNN-based classification study
•	Cross-device validation study (regular fundus vs. UWF fundus)
•	Image quality assessment study
Justification: The paper describes the DeepDRiD challenge (ISBI 2020), providing a new benchmark dataset with three sub-challenges: regular fundus DR grading, image quality assessment, and UWF fundus DR grading. It evaluates 34 teams' submissions using standardized metrics, making it a benchmark descriptor with empirical evaluation of multiple deep learning approaches. The UWF sub-challenge constitutes a cross-device generalization assessment.
3. Research Problem
Specific Problem: Development and benchmarking of deep learning models for (1) DR severity grading from dual-view regular fundus images, (2) fundus image quality assessment (artifact, clarity, field definition, overall quality), and (3) DR grading from ultra-widefield (UWF) fundus images to test cross-device generalizability.

Related to:
•	Generalization: Yes — sub-challenge 3 explicitly tests cross-device transfer from regular to UWF fundus.
•	Preprocessing: Yes — the paper analyzes preprocessing strategies across top teams.
•	Architecture scaling: Partially — multiple backbone architectures compared (EfficientNet, ResNet, SE-ResNeXt, VGG).
•	Clinical deployment: Yes — image quality assessment directly targets screening feasibility.
•	Lesion detection: No — image-level grading only, no lesion-level segmentation in the challenge.
4. Datasets Used
4.1 DeepDRiD Regular Fundus Dataset
Name: DeepDRiD Regular Fundus
Public/Private: Public (released on GitHub/Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.6452623)
Sample Size: 2,000 images from 500 patients (4 images per patient: dual-view, bilateral)
Class Taxonomy: 5-class (ICDR scale: Grade 0–4)
Train/Validation/Test Split: Regular Set-A: 300 patients / 1,200 images (training, 60%); Regular Set-B: 100 patients / 400 images (online validation, 20%); Regular Set-C: 100 patients / 400 images (final testing, 20%)
External Dataset Used: Yes — participants permitted to use external data (Kaggle 2015, APTOS, IDRiD, Messidor, ROC, E-Ophtha, DiaretDB, REFUGE 2)
Cross-Dataset Testing: Not formally required for sub-challenge 1; however, sub-challenge 3 constitutes cross-device evaluation.

4.2 DeepDRiD UWF Fundus Dataset
Name: DeepDRiD Ultra-Widefield (UWF) Fundus
Public/Private: Public
Sample Size: 256 images from 128 patients
Class Taxonomy: 5-class (ICDR scale: Grade 0–4)
Train/Validation/Test Split: UWF Set-A: 77 patients / 154 images; UWF Set-B: 25 patients / 50 images; UWF Set-C: 26 patients / 52 images
Cross-Dataset Testing: Yes — teams transferred knowledge from regular fundus datasets to UWF fundus.
5. Preprocessing Pipeline
Preprocessing varied by team and sub-challenge. The paper reports details for top 3 teams per sub-challenge:

Sub-challenge 1 (Regular Fundus DR Grading)
Rank	Cut	Color	Resize	Filling
1	None	None	None	None
2	Black edge removal	Ben's preprocessing	Bilinear interpolation (512)	None
3	Black edge removal	None	Bilinear interpolation (1,024)	None

Sub-challenge 2 (Image Quality Assessment)
Rank	Cut	Color	Resize	Filling
1	None	None	Bilinear interpolation (512)	None
2	None	None	None	None
3	Black edge removal	None	None	Symmetrical flip fill

Sub-challenge 3 (UWF DR Grading)
Rank	Cut	Color	Resize	Filling
1	Center cut	None	None	None
2	Center cut	None	None	None
3	None	None	None	None

CLAHE: [NOT REPORTED]
Color normalization (explicit method): Ben's preprocessing used by 1 team in sub-challenge 1 (Rank 2)
Augmentation: Reported in separate table (see Section 6 context). Included horizontal/vertical flips, rotation, CutMix, RICAP, Mixup, elastic/grid/affine transforms.
Image quality filtering: Not applied as preprocessing; image quality was the target of sub-challenge 2.
Lesion enhancement: [NOT REPORTED]
6. Model Architecture
Multiple architectures were used across 9 top teams. Key details per sub-challenge:

Sub-challenge 1 (DR Grading)
•	All top 3 teams used EfficientNet as backbone.
•	Loss functions: Smooth L1, Smooth L1 + Cross-Entropy + Dual View + Patient-level loss, L1 + Cross-Entropy (5-class one-vs-others).
•	Pre-training: Kaggle 2015 + APTOS (Rank 1 & 2); labeled + unlabeled datasets (Rank 3).
•	Training strategies: MMoE, GMP, OHEM, cross-validation, oversampling, early stopping (Rank 1); cross-validation + TTA (Rank 2); pseudo-labeled training (Rank 3).

Sub-challenge 2 (Image Quality)
•	Rank 1: SE-ResNeXt; Rank 2: ResNet; Rank 3: VGG + UNet.
•	Loss: Cross-entropy (Rank 1, 3); Cost-sensitive loss + L1 (Rank 2).
•	Pre-training: ImageNet (Rank 1); Kaggle 2015 (Rank 2); private fundus lesion segmentation data (Rank 3).
•	All used transfer learning.

Sub-challenge 3 (UWF DR Grading)
•	All top 3 teams used EfficientNet.
•	Pre-training: labeled + unlabeled data (Rank 1); Kaggle 2015 + APTOS (Rank 2); None (Rank 3).
•	Rank 1 used pseudo-labeled training; Rank 2 used MMoE/GMP/OHEM/CV; Rank 3 used transfer learning.

Input Resolution: 512 or 1,024 pixels (varied by team)
Optimizer: [NOT REPORTED explicitly]
Epochs: [NOT REPORTED]
Hyperparameters: [NOT REPORTED in detail]
7. Validation Design
•	Internal validation: Yes — Set-B served as online validation during competition.
•	External validation (held-out test set): Yes — Set-C used for final evaluation (not seen by participants during development).
•	Cross-validation: Used by some teams internally (e.g., Rank 1 & 2 in sub-challenge 1).
•	External cross-dataset validation: Implicit through sub-challenge 3 (UWF data from different device).
•	Prospective validation: No.
•	Multi-center validation: No — all data from Shanghai-based institutions.
8. Performance Metrics
Sub-challenge 1: Regular Fundus DR Grading (Quadratic Weighted Kappa)
Rank	Team	Affiliation	κw Score
1	Xi Fang et al.	Shanghai Jiao Tong University	0.9303
2	Jiang Li et al.	Shanghai Jiao Tong University	0.9262
3	Jaemin Son et al.	VUNO Inc.	0.9232
Range across all 9 invited teams: 0.9303 to 0.9033.

Sub-challenge 2: Image Quality Assessment (Quadratic Weighted Kappa / Accuracy)
Rank	Team	κw Score	Accuracy Range
1	Poorneshwaran J M et al.	0.6981	0.70
2	Adrian Galdran et al.	0.6950	~0.695
3	Yerui Chen et al.	0.6938	~0.65–0.69
Overall accuracy for image quality ranged from 0.70 to 0.65 across teams.

Sub-challenge 3: UWF Fundus DR Grading (Quadratic Weighted Kappa)
Rank	Team	Affiliation	κw Score
1	Jaemin Son et al.	VUNO Inc.	0.9062
2	Xi Fang et al.	Shanghai Jiao Tong University	0.8620
3	Jie Wang et al.	Beihang University	0.8230
Range across all teams in sub-challenge 3: 0.9062 to 0.6437.

AUC: [NOT REPORTED]
Sensitivity/Specificity: Stated to be comparable with physicians but specific values reported only in supplemental materials (Note S3).
F1 / Cohen's Kappa (unweighted): [NOT REPORTED separately]
Confidence Intervals: [NOT REPORTED]
Statistical Tests: [NOT REPORTED]
 
9. Authors' Claims
Performance Claims
•	Top teams achieved κw of 0.93 for regular DR grading, with sensitivity and specificity comparable to physicians.
•	Image quality assessment accuracy ranged 0.65–0.70, described as insufficient for clinical feasibility.
•	UWF DR grading achieved κw up to 0.9062, demonstrating transfer learning effectiveness from regular to UWF fundus.

Generalization Claims
•	Using regular fundus images for knowledge transfer has a "very significant effect" on DR grading for UWF images.
•	Pre-training with large-scale public datasets improves model generalization.

Clinical Applicability Claims
•	Models can enhance healthcare workers' judgment in DR screening.
•	Image quality assessment remains insufficient for clinically feasible automatic screening.

Superiority Claims
•	No explicit superiority claims over prior work; the paper positions DeepDRiD as a complementary benchmark to existing challenges (IDRiD, Kaggle, Messidor).
10. Empirical Support Assessment
Generalization claims supported by data? Partially. The UWF sub-challenge demonstrates cross-device transfer, but UWF dataset is very small (52 test images from 26 patients). The claim of "very significant effect" is qualitative rather than statistically tested.
External validation robust? Limited. Set-C is held-out but from the same institution and demographic population. No external multi-center or multi-ethnic validation.
Confidence intervals reported? No. No CIs, no p-values, no statistical significance testing.
Dataset size adequate? Regular fundus (2,000 images) is moderate. UWF dataset (256 images) is small. Both are single-center.
Class imbalance addressed? The paper notes class imbalance as a factor in poor image quality assessment results. Some teams used oversampling and cost-sensitive loss, but systematic class distribution reporting is limited.
Statistical testing adequate? No. No formal statistical comparison between teams or between models and human performance.
11. Internal Validity
Overfitting Risk: Moderate. The competition structure with separate validation (Set-B) and test (Set-C) sets mitigates overfitting. However, 3 allowed submissions could permit minor overfitting to the test set.
Dataset Leakage Risk: Low for the challenge format. Patient-level splits prevent image-level leakage. However, participants were allowed to use external datasets, creating potential indirect leakage if overlapping patients exist.
Confounders: Single-center data introduces demographic/device confounders. Annotation subjectivity (especially for image quality) is acknowledged.
Augmentation Inflation Risk: Low. Augmentation varied across teams and was documented. Performance was evaluated on non-augmented test data.
Metric Reliability: Quadratic weighted kappa is appropriate for ordinal DR grading. Accuracy alone for image quality is limited given class imbalance.
12. External Validity
Cross-Population Transferability: Unknown. All data from Shanghai, China. No multi-ethnic or multi-geographic validation. Age distribution skews elderly (mean ~61–75 years).
Dataset Portability: Good. Dataset is publicly released. ICDR 5-class taxonomy is standard.
Clinical Feasibility: Authors explicitly state image quality assessment performance is insufficient for clinical deployment. DR grading performance is promising but single-center.
Hardware Constraints: [NOT REPORTED]
13. Strengths
•	Publicly released, dual-view (macula-centered + optic disc-centered) dataset is unique and clinically relevant.
•	Simultaneous DR grading + image quality assessment enables joint investigation of both tasks.
•	UWF fundus sub-challenge provides a rare benchmark for cross-device generalizability.
•	Detailed annotation protocol with multi-reader (2 junior + 1 senior ophthalmologist) adjudication.
•	Comprehensive reporting of preprocessing, augmentation, pre-training, and training strategies across teams.
•	Patient-level splits prevent image-level data leakage.
•	Challenge format ensures fair comparison under standardized evaluation.
14. Limitations
Explicit (Stated by Authors)
•	Annotation subjectivity, especially for image quality, is a limiting factor.
•	Competition had only 1.5 months for method development.
•	Image quality assessment results did not reach clinically feasible performance.

Implicit (Methodological)
•	Single-center data from Shanghai limits demographic and device generalizability.
•	UWF dataset is very small (256 images, 52 test images) — insufficient for robust generalization conclusions.
•	No confidence intervals or statistical significance tests reported.
•	No AUC, sensitivity, or specificity reported in the main paper (deferred to supplemental).
•	No Vision Transformer architectures compared (all submissions pre-ViT era / ViT not adopted by participants).
•	Accuracy as sole metric for image quality assessment is inadequate given class imbalance.
•	No lesion-level evaluation — only image-level grading.
•	External data usage was permitted but not standardized, confounding fair comparison.
15. Relevance to My Dissertation
Preprocessing Dominance Hypothesis: Moderately relevant. The paper explicitly documents that the winning team in sub-challenge 1 (DR grading) did NOT use complex preprocessing/augmentation but relied on advanced training strategies. In contrast, sub-challenge 2 winner used rich preprocessing. This provides nuanced evidence: preprocessing dominance may be task-dependent rather than universal.
Cross-Database Validation: Relevant. Sub-challenge 3 (UWF) represents a form of cross-device/cross-modality validation, though it is not cross-database in the traditional sense (e.g., training on EyePACS, testing on Messidor).
EyePACS/Messidor Benchmarking: Indirectly relevant. Multiple teams used Kaggle 2015 (EyePACS) and Messidor for pre-training, but performance on those datasets is not reported. The DeepDRiD dataset itself is positioned as a complementary benchmark.
Vision Transformer Comparison: Not relevant. No ViT-based architectures were submitted. Challenge predates widespread ViT adoption in DR grading.
Risk of Contradiction: Low. The paper's finding that the top DR grading team did not rely on heavy preprocessing could nuance (but not directly contradict) a preprocessing-dominance argument, since the winner instead leveraged training strategy innovations (MMoE, OHEM, oversampling).
16. Citation-Ready Statements
1. The DeepDRiD challenge (ISBI 2020) provided 2,000 regular fundus images from 500 patients and 256 UWF images from 128 patients, both annotated for DR grading and image quality (Liu et al., 2022, p. 1).
2. The top-performing algorithm for regular fundus DR grading achieved a quadratic weighted kappa of 0.9303 on the held-out test set (Liu et al., 2022, Table 8, p. 9).
3. Image quality assessment accuracy ranged from 0.65 to 0.70 across top teams, which the authors stated did not reach clinically feasible performance (Liu et al., 2022, p. 7).
4. In the UWF DR grading sub-challenge, knowledge transfer from regular fundus datasets demonstrated a "very significant effect," with the top team achieving κw = 0.9062 (Liu et al., 2022, p. 7).
5. The winning team for regular fundus DR grading did not use complex preprocessing or augmentation but employed advanced training strategies including multi-gate mixture of experts and online hard example mining (Liu et al., 2022, p. 5).
6. EfficientNet was the dominant backbone architecture, adopted by most top-performing teams across all three sub-challenges (Liu et al., 2022, Table 7, p. 8).
7. All data originated from Shanghai-based institutions, and annotation subjectivity (particularly for image quality) was acknowledged as a limiting factor (Liu et al., 2022, p. 7).
17. Epistemic Classification
Classification: Benchmark Study

Justification: This paper establishes the DeepDRiD dataset and challenge as a new benchmark for DR grading and image quality assessment. It provides standardized evaluation of multiple competing algorithms under fair conditions. The publicly released dataset with dual-view annotations and quality labels makes it a reusable resource. However, it does not introduce novel architectures or advance theoretical understanding; it catalogs and compares existing approaches. The single-center origin and absence of statistical testing limit its epistemic weight for generalization claims.
18. Analytical Synthesis
Liu et al. (2022) present the DeepDRiD challenge as a benchmark descriptor that contributes a publicly available, dual-view fundus dataset with both DR grading and image quality annotations. The epistemic weight of this paper is moderate: it demonstrates that EfficientNet-based architectures with sophisticated training strategies (rather than preprocessing) dominate DR grading on this dataset, achieving strong agreement with expert labels (κw = 0.93). The image quality assessment results (≤0.70 accuracy) highlight a significant gap in automated DR screening pipelines, reinforcing the argument that image quality is a critical bottleneck. For dissertation positioning, the paper provides nuanced evidence regarding the preprocessing-dominance hypothesis: the top DR grading team explicitly avoided complex preprocessing, succeeding instead through training strategy innovations. This suggests preprocessing may be necessary but not sufficient, and that its relative importance depends on the downstream task. The UWF sub-challenge offers preliminary evidence of cross-device transferability, though the very small test set (52 images) limits the robustness of this finding. Critically, the single-center origin and absence of confidence intervals or statistical testing weaken generalization claims. The dataset's value as a benchmark is clear, but it does not substitute for cross-population external validation studies.

— End of Literature Card —
